## Editorial style guide

### References
Follow Merriam-Webster and AP style for questions not covered here.

## Abbreviations and acronyms

### First use
Spell out unfamiliar abbreviations on first reference, then use the short form.

### Common forms
Use standard abbreviations such as a.m., p.m., i.e., e.g., and etc.

## Buttons

### Button text
Use sentence case in call-to-action buttons.

## Capitalization

### General rule
Use common sense. When in doubt, do not capitalize.

### Job titles
Lowercase job titles unless they directly precede a name as part of a formal title.

## Commas

### Serial comma
Use the Oxford comma in a series.

### Restrictive clauses
Use "that" for restrictive clauses and "which" for nonrestrictive clauses.

## Headlines

### Title case
Use title case for headlines and sentence case for subheadings.

## Linking

### Link text
Link the useful words in a sentence. Avoid "click here."

## Numbers

### General rule
Spell out one through nine unless precision matters. Use numerals for 10 and above.

### Percentages
Use numerals with the word "percent."

## Pronouns

### Singular they
Use singular "they" for generic references.

## Punctuation

### Exclamation points
Use exclamation points sparingly.

### Semicolons
Avoid semicolons in product copy unless they improve clarity.

## Word bank

### Standard terms
Use: open source, startup, homepage, login (noun), log in (verb), username, large language model, well-being, Wi-Fi.
